content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 307 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance Administration • • • Executive Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest

(916) 322 5660          322 5901          322 6441          322 6444


January 27, 1978


Steven V. Seekins
Coordinator
Senate Republican Caucus
1116 Ninth Street, Suite 68
Sacramento, CA  95814

Dear Mr. Seekins:

     The Fair Political Practices Commission has received
your letter inquiring whether a Senate employee can do research
work, on his or her own time, for compensation for a private
firm.  The research work being done may be political in nature.

     The Political Reform Act does not address the question
of what activities state employees may perform on their non-
state time.  Therefore no comment can be made by the Commission
on the propriety of such a plan.

     However if the individual who will be performing
this work is a designated employee under the Senate's Conflict
of Interest Code, the private firm may be a source of income
which must be disclosed on the employee's Statement of Economic
Interests.  If the employee does have a reportable interest
in that income from the private firm, the employee will have
to disqualify himself or herself from making decisions which
could have a material financial effect on that source of
income.

     Your letter specified the individual would be per-
forming the work on non-state time.  You should note that our
response would differ if the individual were performing the
work on state time.  The Commission has made it clear that it
believes that the pro rata share of the salary of a state
legislator employee who spends greater than 10% of his time
in one calendar month performing campaign activities on state
time is reportable as a contribution.  See Government Code
Section 82015 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections 18215 and 18423.

Steven V. Seekins
January 27, 1978
Page Two


The Commission took this position in the lawsuit
which it filed on November 17, 1977, in Sacramento Superior
Court against the Friends of Tom Suitt, et al. For your
information, I am enclosing a copy of that complaint.

That position was not adopted by the Sacramento
Superior Court. In a related action, Friends of Tom Suitt v.
FPPC, Civ. No. 27025, Superior Court Judge Joseph DeCristoforo
on January 5, 1978, issued a memorandum of decision stating
that the California State Legislature was not a "person"
under the Political Reform Act and that, therefore, payment
by the Legislature of salary to an employee for campaign work
could not be considered a reportable contribution. That
Superior Court decision also enjoins the Commission from
pursuing the lawsuit it filed against Friends of Tom Suitt.
The Commission will appeal Judge DeCristoforo's ruling.

I hope this information has been helpful to you.
If you have any further questions, please contact me.

Sincerely,

Barbara Campbell
Counsel
Legal Division

BC:plh
Enclosure

746002

PETER H. BEHR
ROSS W G. BEVERLY
JOHN V. BRIGGS
WILLIAM CAMPBELL
LOU CUSANOVICH
GEORGE DEUKMEJIAN
RAY JOHNSON

MILTON MARKS
JOHN A. NEJEDLY
ROBERT NIMMO
H. L. RICHARDSON
NEWTON R. RUSSELL
JOHN STULL

California Legislature

SENATE REPUBLICAN CAUCUS

DENNIS E. CARPENTER
CHAIRMAN

STEVEN V. SEEKINS
COORDINATOR

1116 NINTH STREET, SUITE 68
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
TELEPHONE: (916) 445-6617

December 10, 1977

Mr. Dan Lowenstein
Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Mr. Lowenstein:

Recently a member of a Senator's legislative staff has
inquired about the following question on which I would
appreciate your decision and written response:

     Can a Senate employee on his or her own time
     do research work which may from time to time
     be political in nature for a private sector
     firm known for its political campaign manage-
     ment skills? This firm also handles commercial
     accounts and any work done by the Senate
     employee would be done for compensation and
     on the employee's own time.

Since your response will become the basis for a policy
decision, your judgment as to the degree of formality of
your opinion is welcomed.

Thank you for your assistance.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]

                                        STEVEN V. SEEKINS
                                        Coordinator

SVS:jc

The research work being done may be political in nature. The Political Reform Act does not address the question of what activities state employees may perform on their non-state time. Therefore no comment can be made by the Commission on the propriety of such a plan. However if the individual who will be performing this work is a designated employee under the Senate's Conflict of Interest Code, the private firm may be a source of income which must be disclosed on the employee's Statement of Economic Interests. If the employee does have a reportable interest in that income from the private firm, the employee will have to disqualify himself or herself from making decisions which could have a material financial effect on that source of income. Your letter specified the individual would be performing the work on non-state time. You should note that our response would differ if the individual were performing the work on state time. The Commission has made it clear that it believes that the pro rata share of the salary of a state legislative employee who spends greater than 10% of his time in one calendar month performing campaign activities on state time is reportable as a contribution. See Government Code Section 82015 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections 18215 and 18423. The Commission took this position in the lawsuit which it filed on November 17, 1977, in Sacramento Superior Court against the Friends of Tom Suitt, et al. For your information, I am enclosing a copy of that complaint. That position was not adopted by the Sacramento Superior Court. In a related action, Friends of Tom Suitt v. FPPC, Civ. No. 27025, Superior Court Judge Joseph DeCristoforo on January 5, 1978, issued a memorandum of decision stating that the California State Legislature was not a "person" under the Political Reform Act and that, therefore, payment by the Legislature of salary to an employee for campaign work could not be considered a reportable contribution. That Superior Court decision also enjoins the Commission from pursuing the lawsuit it filed against Friends of Tom Suitt. The Commission will appeal Judge DeCristoforo's ruling. I hope this information has been helpful to you. If you have any further questions, please contact me.
